name: Mente Pass – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees
name_original: Circuit 8 : Le col de Menté — Les cols emblématiques des Pyrénées
description:
The Menté is one of the most used passes by the Tour de France in the Pyrenees. Since 1966, the big loop has taken it twenty times. Linking the Ger valley to that of the Garonne, it rises to 1,349 meters above sea level.

The start of the loop that leads to this classic starts from the charming mountain village of Fronsac.
About ten kilometers at the bottom of the valley allow you to warm up before attacking the steep slopes of the pass. Indeed, the average slope is 9% over a little over nine kilometers with some passages beyond 11%. The last part of the climb is done by means of several switchbacks. At the top you will discover a stele in memory of Serge Lapébie, a former professional cyclist.

The descent requires a certain vigilance because of the many laces that wind through the forest. At the Oule bridge, the slope becomes gentler and you move through a remarkably peaceful valley bottom. You follow the Ger for a long time before leaving it to attack the small Col de Buret. You then go on to the Col des Ares, a little longer, which is the last ascent of the route. The descent then brings you back to the heights of Fronsac.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Fronsac traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, expansive vineyards, and the confluence of the Isle and Dordogne rivers. The region features a mix of marshlands, hedgerows, and ancient residences, offering varied scenery. Elevations range from gentle slopes to climbs exceeding 1500 feet, accommodating different fitness levels. This diverse terrain provides a compelling environment for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Fronsac The most popular road cycling route is Mente Pass – emblematic passes of the Pyrenees, a 36.1 miles (58.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Bagnères-de-Luchon – Superbagnères Summit (1804 m) loop from Marignac - Saint-Béat, a difficult 43.7 miles (70.4 km) path. This route ascends to a high summit, offering challenging climbs and panoramic views. Local road cyclists also love the Col des Ares – Buret Pass loop from Marignac - Saint-Béat, a 37.6 miles (60.5 km) trail leading through varied mountain passes, often completed in about 3 hours 1 minute. Road cycling around Fronsac is defined by rolling hills, extensive vineyards, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides through the countryside to more challenging routes with significant elevation. The routes in Fronsac are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 111 reviews. More than 4000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Fronsac's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Fronsac?
A: Fronsac offers a diverse landscape for road cyclists, characterized by rolling hills, expansive vineyards, and the confluence of the Isle and Dordogne rivers. You'll encounter a mix of marshlands, hedgerows, and picturesque country roads, mostly paved. The topography includes varying elevations, from gentle slopes to more challenging climbs exceeding 1500 feet, catering to different fitness levels.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Fronsac?
A: Yes, Fronsac has routes suitable for beginners. While some routes feature significant elevation, others offer gentler slopes with around 500 feet of elevation gain, making them accessible for those new to road cycling. The region's network includes 9 easy routes, perfect for a relaxed ride through the scenic countryside.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Fronsac region?
A: There are over 60 road cycling routes available in the Fronsac region. This includes a variety of options, with 9 easy routes, 27 moderate routes, and 28 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for every skill level.

Q: What are the best times of year for road cycling in Fronsac?
A: The Fronsac region is ideal for road cycling during spring and autumn when the weather is mild and the landscapes are particularly vibrant. Spring brings blooming vineyards, while autumn offers stunning fall colors. Summer can also be pleasant, though it's advisable to ride earlier in the morning or later in the afternoon to avoid the midday heat.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through vineyards in Fronsac?
A: Absolutely. Many road cycling routes in Fronsac wind directly through the famed vineyards, offering opportunities to combine cycling with wine tourism. The region is known for its 840-hectare viticultural garden, and routes often pass by renowned appellations like Pomerol and Saint-Émilion, providing picturesque vineyard views.

Q: What cultural attractions or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes in Fronsac?
A: Fronsac's road cycling routes often lead past historic châteaux, ancient residences, and religious sites like Saint-Christophe Church. A major highlight easily accessible from the region is the magnificent medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/128890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Émilion Village&lt;/a&gt;, a UNESCO World Heritage Site surrounded by vineyards, offering a unique cultural experience.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling loops in Fronsac?
A: Yes, the Fronsac region offers numerous circular road cycling loops. These routes are designed to bring you back to your starting point, making planning easier. Many of these loops traverse the picturesque countryside and vineyards, providing varied scenery without needing to retrace your path.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the Fronsac region?
A: The Fronsac region is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 111 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the picturesque landscapes, the variety of terrain from rolling hills to vineyards, and the charm of the historic towns and villages encountered along the routes.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs in Fronsac?
A: Yes, for experienced road cyclists seeking a challenge, Fronsac offers routes with significant elevation gains. Some paths feature climbs exceeding 1500 feet, providing a demanding workout amidst the region's varied topography. There are 28 difficult routes available for those looking for a strenuous ride.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Fronsac?
A: While specific parking areas for each route vary, many routes in Fronsac start from or pass through charming towns and villages. These locations often provide public parking facilities suitable for cyclists. It's advisable to check local town maps or tourist information for designated parking spots near your chosen starting point.

Q: Are there quiet road cycling paths in Fronsac, away from heavy traffic?
A: Yes, Fronsac is known for its network of picturesque country roads, many of which are quiet and less trafficked. These paths wind through vineyards, marshlands, and hedgerows, offering a peaceful cycling experience away from busier main roads. The region's focus on viticulture means many roads are designed for local access rather than high-volume traffic.
tips: Saint Béat is a very typical French village. The village maintains its architectural style throughout, and it is deeply rooted in the landscape. You can find bars and a few other shops.

The Col de Menté connects the Garonne valley to that of the Ger, culminating at an altitude of 1,349 meters. Its ascent from the village of Saint-Béat is 9.3 kilometers long with an average of 9.13%, including some passages at more than 11%. It is also possible to climb there from the Oule bridge. The climb extends over 10.9 kilometers with an average of 6.6%.

Small easy pass after Aspet to the Ares pass.
The pass is the starting point for beautiful hikes towards the Cagire.
The Col des Ares culminates at 797 meters above sea level. Located on the heights of the village of Fronsac, it offers a lovely panorama over the Garonne valley.

waypoints.name: View of the Château de Saint-Béat
